items.title: Growth Requirements of Haemophilus influenzae
items.content: H. influenzae is a small, gram-negative pleomorphic coccobacillus that requires specific conditions for growth. It needs blood-containing media that has been preheated to 80°C (176°F) to grow effectively on chocolate agar plates due to its need for hemin and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide.
keyPoints: H. influenzae is a pleomorphic coccobacillus.
Requires blood-containing media that has been preheated to 80°C (176°F) for growth on chocolate agar plates.
Needs hemin (X factor) and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (V factor).
MALDI-ToF is widely used, but phenotypic methods still apply in some labs.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY

items.title: Clinical Relevance of H. influenzae Serotypes
items.content: H. influenzae has six major serotypes (a-f) based on antigenically distinct polysaccharide capsules, with type b and nontypeable strains being most clinically significant. Type b primarily affects infants and children under 6 years old but is now rare due to vaccination.
keyPoints: Six major serotypes of H. influenzae: a-f based on capsular antigens.
Type b and nontypeable strains are the most clinically relevant.
Hib primarily affects infants and children <6 years, now rare due to vaccines.
Nontypeable strains cause mucosal infections but can be invasive in immunocompromised hosts.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY

items.title: Impact of Hib Vaccination on Epidemiology of H. influenzae Infections
items.content: The introduction of Hib conjugate vaccines has significantly reduced nasopharyngeal colonization and incidence of invasive Hib disease in many industrialized countries, particularly among children who are unimmunized or have not completed the primary immunization series.
keyPoints: 30-60% of healthy preschool children are intermittent carriers of nontypeable H. influenzae.
Striking decreases in nasopharyngeal colonization and incidence of Hib infection due to widespread use of Hib conjugate vaccines.
Invasive Hib disease predominantly occurs in unimmunized children or those who have not completed the primary immunization series globally.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND TRANSMISSION | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Pathogenesis of H. influenzae Infections and Factors Influencing Disease Incidence
items.content: Hib strains cause systemic disease through invasion and hematogenous spread, facilitated by the type b polysaccharide capsule which helps avoid opsonization. Nontypeable strains cause local infections like otitis media and recurrent respiratory infections in adults with COPD, often forming biofilms that contribute to chronicity.
keyPoints: Type b polysaccharide capsule is a key virulence factor for Hib causing systemic disease.
Nontypeable H. influenzae causes otitis media by local invasion of mucosal surfaces and forms biofilms in the middle ear, leading to chronic infections.
Increased incidence of invasive disease caused by nontypeable strains may be due to previous oversight during the pre-vaccine era.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND TRANSMISSION | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Importance of Antibody Response in Protecting Against Hib Infections
items.content: Antibodies to the capsule, specifically against polyribitol ribose phosphate (PRP), are crucial for protection from Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) infections. Maternally acquired antibodies decline significantly by 6 months of age and remain low until about 2-3 years unless vaccination occurs. This correlates with peak incidence rates of Hib disease in young children. Conjugate vaccines that link PRP to protein carriers have been developed, effectively generating an antibody response even in infants and preventing invasive infections.
keyPoints: Antibody to the capsule is crucial for protection against Hib.
Maternally acquired serum antibody levels decline from birth to 6 months of age.
In the absence of vaccination, antibody levels remain low until ~2 or 3 years of age.
The peak incidence of type b disease correlates with the antibody nadir.
Conjugate vaccines generate an effective antibody response in infants.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · B | IMMUNE RESPONSE

items.title: Immune Response to Nontypeable H. influenzae Strains
items.content: Nontypeable strains of Haemophilus influenzae, which lack a capsule, elicit an immune response against noncapsular antigens. This response is strain-specific and contributes to the tendency of these strains to cause recurrent otitis media and exacerbations of chronic bronchitis in immunocompetent individuals.
keyPoints: Nontypeable H. influenzae strains lack a capsule, directing immune response towards noncapsular antigens.
The immune response is strain-specific for nontypeable strains.
This specificity contributes to recurrent otitis media and exacerbations of chronic bronchitis.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · B | IMMUNE RESPONSE

items.title: Hib Meningitis: Key Features and Complications
items.content: Hib meningitis primarily affects children under 2 years, presenting with fever and altered CNS function. Subdural effusion is a common complication if seizures or hemiparesis persist despite antibiotics.
keyPoints: Affects children <2 years of age
Common features: Fever, altered CNS function
Subdural effusion suspected with persistent seizures/hemiparesis after 2-3 days of antibiotics
Mortality rate ~5%
6% permanent sensorineural hearing loss among survivors
25% significant disability in survivors
Up to 50% have neurologic sequelae like partial hearing loss or delayed language development
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Epiglottitis: Demographics and Presentation
items.content: Hib epiglottitis, a life-threatening condition, occurs in older children (2-7 years) and is absent among Navajo Native Americans and Alaskan Eskimos. It progresses rapidly from sore throat to airway obstruction.
keyPoints: Occurs in 2–7 year olds
Absent in Navajo Native Americans and Alaskan Eskimos
Progresses from sore throat, fever to dysphagia, drooling, airway obstruction
Primarily affects adults in countries with Hib vaccine programs
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Nontypeable H. influenzae: Common Infections and Implications
items.content: Nontypeable H. influenzae is a frequent cause of COPD exacerbations, community-acquired pneumonia in adults, and childhood otitis media. It's also linked to puerperal sepsis and neonatal bacteremia.
keyPoints: Most common bacterial cause of COPD exacerbations
Characterized by increased cough, sputum production, shortness of breath
Causes community-acquired pneumonia in adults with COPD or immunocompromised conditions
One of three most common causes of childhood otitis media (others: S. pneumoniae, Moraxella catarrhalis)
Associated with recurrent episodes, treatment failure, and recent antimicrobial therapy
Causes puerperal sepsis and neonatal bacteremia
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Invasive Nontypeable H. influenzae Infections
items.content: Nontypeable H. influenzae is a significant cause of various invasive infections, especially in regions with widespread Hib vaccination. These include bacteremia, empyema, and more. Many patients have underlying conditions like HIV or alcoholism.
keyPoints: Nontypeable H. influenzae causes sinusitis and various invasive infections such as bacteremia, empyema, adult epiglottitis, pericarditis, cellulitis, septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, endocarditis, cholecystitis, intraabdominal infections, urinary tract infections, mastoiditis, aortic graft infection.
Most invasive H. influenzae infections in Hib-vaccinated countries are caused by nontypeable strains.
Many patients with H. influenzae bacteremia have underlying conditions like HIV, cardiopulmonary disease, alcoholism, or cancer.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnosing Invasive H. influenzae Infections
items.content: The definitive diagnosis of invasive H. influenzae infection relies on culturing the organism from normally sterile sites like blood or CSF. PCR and DNA-based methods are also used for rapid identification.
keyPoints: Definitive diagnosis requires recovery of H. influenzae in culture from a normally sterile site (blood, CSF, joint fluid).
Rapid PCR and other DNA-based methods aid in identifying H. influenzae.

Distinguishing nontypeable H. influenzae from Haemophilus haemolyticus involves genotypic markers like 16S ribosomal sequences, superoxide dismutase, outer-membrane protein P6, protein D, and fuculose kinase.
Gram-negative coccobacilli in Gram-stained CSF strongly suggest Hib meningitis; recovery or detection of H. influenzae DNA confirms it.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Initial Therapy for Hib Meningitis: Cephalosporins and Glucocorticoids
items.content: For Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) meningitis, initial therapy should include a cephalosporin such as ceftriaxone or cefotaxime. In children, ceftriaxone is dosed at 75–100 mg/kg daily in two doses every 12 hours, while cefotaxime requires 2 g every 4-6 hours for adults. An alternative regimen includes ampicillin plus chloramphenicol. Additionally, administering glucocorticoids like dexamethasone can reduce neurologic sequelae by mitigating inflammation.
keyPoints: Cephalosporin therapy: Ceftriaxone (75–100 mg/kg in children; 2 g every 12 hours for adults) or cefotaxime (2 g every 4-6 hours for adults).
Alternative regimen: Ampicillin (200–300 mg/kg daily) + chloramphenicol (75–100 mg/kg daily), both in four divided doses.
Glucocorticoid therapy: Dexamethasone (0.6 mg/kg per day IV, 4 doses for 2 days) recommended for children >2 months with Hib meningitis to reduce neurologic sequelae.
Duration of therapy: 1–2 weeks.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · Haemophilus influenzae

items.title: Treatment and Resistance Patterns in Nontypeable H. influenzae Infections
items.content: Nontypeable strains of H. influenzae, responsible for infections like otitis media, sinusitis, and COPD exacerbations, are often treated with oral antimicrobials. Approximately 30-40% produce $\beta$-lactamase, conferring resistance to ampicillin and amoxicillin. Effective treatments include amoxicillin/clavulanic acid, extended-spectrum cephalosporins, macrolides (azithromycin, clarithromycin), and fluoroquinolones for adults with COPD exacerbations. However, fluoroquinolones are not recommended for children or pregnant women due to potential effects on articular cartilage.
keyPoints: 30-40% of nontypeable H. influenzae strains produce $\beta$-lactamase, leading to resistance against ampicillin and amoxicillin.
Effective treatments: Amoxicillin/clavulanic acid, extended-spectrum cephalosporins, macrolides (azithromycin, clarithromycin), fluoroquinolones for adults with COPD exacerbations.
Fluoroquinolone caution: Not recommended for children or pregnant women due to potential effects on articular cartilage.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · Haemophilus influenzae

items.title: Hib Vaccination Schedule and Cost-Effectiveness
items.content: The use of Hib conjugate vaccines is crucial for preventing invasive infections in infants and children. These vaccines not only elicit protective antibodies but also reduce pharyngeal colonization with Hib, significantly lowering disease incidence.
keyPoints: Three monovalent conjugate vaccines licensed in the U.S. prevent invasive Hib infections.
Vaccination reduces rates of pharyngeal colonization and is cost-effective despite high manufacturing costs.
First dose at ~2 months; primary series at 2–6 months; booster at 12–15 months.
Routine vaccination has reduced disease burden in countries like The Gambia and Chile.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · ☐ PREVENTION

items.title: Chemoprophylaxis for Hib Disease Contacts
items.content: Prophylactic measures are recommended to prevent secondary Hib infections among household contacts, especially when there is an incompletely immunized child under 4 years old.
keyPoints: Oral rifampin prophylaxis is indicated for all household contacts if there's an index case with at least one incompletely immunized contact <4 years.
Rifampin administration is also recommended in child-care facilities when two or more cases occur within 60 days among incompletely vaccinated children.
Chemoprophylaxis is not needed for nursery and child-care contacts of a single index case.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Chancroid and HIV Transmission Risk
items.content: Chancroid, caused by Haemophilus ducreyi, is associated with increased risk of HIV transmission due to genital ulceration.
keyPoints: H. ducreyi causes chancroid, characterized by genital ulceration and inguinal adenitis.
Genital ulcers from chancroid increase the efficiency of HIV transmission and susceptibility.
Chancroid is associated with higher HIV transmission risk.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Epidemiology of Chancroid
items.content: Chancroid is more prevalent in developing countries and associated with certain risk factors.
keyPoints: Prevalence has declined over the past decade and a half, especially in developed countries.
More common in developing regions like South Pacific and Africa.
Transmission predominantly heterosexual; male to female ratio during outbreaks ranges from 3:1 to 25:1.
Associated with contact with commercial sex workers and illicit drug use.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.title: Clinical Features and Differential Diagnosis of Chancroid
items.content: Chancroid presents with specific clinical features that can mimic other conditions.
keyPoints: Incubation period is 4–7 days, followed by a papule evolving into a pustule and then an ulcer.
Ulcers are painful, bleed easily, and may have enlarged tender inguinal lymph nodes.
Differential diagnosis includes primary syphilis, genital herpes, donovanosis, and lymphogranuloma venereum.
Non-sexually transmitted cutaneous ulcers by H. ducreyi can resemble yaws.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Laboratory Confirmation and Diagnostic Criteria for Chancroid
items.content: Chancroid diagnosis is challenging due to clinical inaccuracies, necessitating laboratory confirmation. Culture of H. ducreyi from lesions or aspirates of suppurative lymph nodes using selective media is essential. PCR can also be used in larger labs. A probable diagnosis involves identifying painful genital ulcers with no evidence of T. pallidum infection, a typical chancroid presentation, and negative herpes simplex virus tests.
keyPoints: Culture of H. ducreyi from lesions or aspirates is necessary for accurate diagnosis.
Selective and supplemented media are required due to difficulty in growing the organism.
PCR is an alternative diagnostic tool available in larger clinical laboratories.

Probable diagnosis criteria: painful genital ulcers, no T. pallidum infection (negative dark-field examination or serologic test after 7 days), typical chancroid presentation, and negative herpes simplex virus test.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Haemophilus ducreyi

items.title: Considerations for Chancroid in HIV-Seropositive Patients
items.content: In patients with HIV infection, chancroid healing may be delayed, requiring extended treatment durations. Clinical failure to respond might indicate co-infection, particularly with herpes simplex virus. It is crucial to identify and treat contacts of chancroid patients who had sexual contact within 10 days before symptom onset, regardless of symptoms.
keyPoints: HIV-seropositive patients may experience slow healing from chancroid.
Longer treatment courses might be necessary for HIV-infected individuals.
Clinical failure in these patients could indicate co-infection with herpes simplex virus.
Contacts should be treated if they had sexual contact within 10 days before the patient's symptom onset.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Haemophilus ducreyi

items.title: Understanding M. catarrhalis Colonization and Its Epidemiological Impact
items.content: M. catarrhalis is a gram-negative diplococcus that colonizes the human respiratory tract, with colonization rates in infancy ranging from 33 to 100%. Geographic variation in these rates can be attributed to factors like living conditions and hygiene. The use of pneumococcal conjugate vaccines has led to changes in nasopharyngeal colonization patterns, increasing M. catarrhalis prevalence.
keyPoints: M. catarrhalis is an unencapsulated gram-negative diplococcus.
Colonization rates in infancy range between 33 and 100%.
Geographic variation in colonization rates is influenced by living conditions, day-care attendance, hygiene, household smoking, and population genetics.
Pneumococcal conjugate vaccines have altered nasopharyngeal colonization patterns, increasing M. catarrhalis prevalence.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Pathogenesis of M. catarrhalis Respiratory Infections
items.content: M. catarrhalis causes mucosal infections in the respiratory tract through contiguous spread from its colonizing site in the upper airway, often following a preceding event that disrupts normal defenses.
keyPoints: M. catarrhalis causes mucosal infections by spreading contiguously from the upper airway.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS

options: The media must contain preheated blood.
The media should be kept at room temperature.
The media must lack any blood components.
items.explanation: H. influenzae requires the media to contain preheated blood for growth, as it grows on chocolate agar plates where the blood is slowly heated.

options: The level of maternally acquired serum antibody to PRP declines during this period.
Vaccination against Hib is typically administered after this age range.
Infants develop a natural immunity to Hib strains at this age.

items.explanation: The decline in maternally acquired serum antibody to PRP correlates with the peak incidence of type b disease.

items.question: Why is systemic Hib disease unusual after the age of 6 years?
options: Children develop a natural immunity to all bacterial infections.
The presence of protective antibody prevents invasive infections.
Hib bacteria are less virulent in older children.
Vaccination schedules change significantly after this age.
items.explanation: Protective antibodies prevent systemic Hib disease after the age of 6 years.

items.question: What characteristic of nontypeable H. influenzae strains contributes to recurrent infections in immunocompetent hosts?
options: They have a strong capsule that evades the immune system.
The immune response is directed at noncapsular antigens, which are strain-specific.
These strains mutate rapidly, leading to new variants.
Nontypeable strains produce toxins that suppress the immune response.
items.explanation: The strain-specific immune response to noncapsular antigens contributes to recurrent infections.

items.question: What is the primary route of infection for M. catarrhalis causing mucosal infections?
items.explanation: M. catarrhalis causes mucosal infections by contiguous spread from its colonizing site in the upper airway.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: H. influenzae was first recognized as the cause of influenza.
items.explanation: Pfeiffer erroneously concluded that H. influenzae caused influenza, but it does not.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY

items.statement: Nontypeable strains of H. influenzae are primarily mucosal pathogens.
items.explanation: Nontypeable strains are mainly mucosal pathogens but can cause invasive disease in certain populations.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY

items.statement: The incidence of invasive disease caused by nontypeable H. influenzae has decreased over the past decade.
items.explanation: The passage states that the incidence appears to have increased over the past decade.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND TRANSMISSION | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Nontypeable H. influenzae strains cause recurrent lower respiratory tract infections in adults with COPD.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that adults with COPD experience recurrent lower respiratory tract infection due to nontypeable strains.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND TRANSMISSION | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Vaccines conjugating PRP to protein carriers are effective in preventing invasive Hib infections in infants.
items.explanation: These vaccines generate an antibody response to PRP, effectively preventing invasive infections.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · B | IMMUNE RESPONSE

items.statement: Nontypeable H. influenzae strains have a capsule that is targeted by the immune system.
items.explanation: Nontypeable strains lack a capsule, so the immune response targets noncapsular antigens.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · B | IMMUNE RESPONSE

items.statement: Hib causes pneumonia in infants, which can be clinically distinguished from other types of bacterial pneumonia.
items.explanation: Hib pneumonia is clinically indistinguishable from other types of bacterial pneumonia except for a higher likelihood of pleural involvement.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Nontypeable H. influenzae is one of the three most common causes of childhood otitis media.
items.explanation: Nontypeable H. influenzae, along with S. pneumoniae and Moraxella catarrhalis, are the three most common bacterial causes of childhood otitis media.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Most patients with H. influenzae bacteremia have no underlying condition.
items.explanation: Many patients with H. influenzae bacteremia have an underlying condition such as HIV infection, cardiopulmonary disease, alcoholism, or cancer.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: The presence of gram-negative coccobacilli in Gram-stained CSF is strong evidence for Hib meningitis.
items.explanation: Gram-negative coccobacilli in Gram-stained CSF strongly indicate Hib meningitis.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Approximately 30–40% of nontypeable strains of H. influenzae produce $\beta$-lactamase.
items.explanation: The passage states that approximately 30–40% of nontypeable strains produce $\beta$-lactamase.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · Haemophilus influenzae

items.statement: Resistance to macrolides in H. influenzae is decreasing globally.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that resistance to macrolides is being observed with increasing frequency globally.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · Haemophilus influenzae

items.statement: The widespread use of Hib conjugate vaccines has increased the incidence of Hib disease in developed countries.
items.explanation: The widespread use of Hib conjugate vaccines has dramatically reduced the incidence of Hib disease in developed countries.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · ☐ PREVENTION

items.statement: Chemoprophylaxis is indicated for nursery and child-care contacts of a single index case.
items.explanation: Chemoprophylaxis is not indicated in nursery and child-care contacts of a single index case.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: Chancroid is more common in developed countries than developing countries.
items.explanation: Chancroid appears to be more common in developing countries.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.statement: Infection with Haemophilus ducreyi confers immunity against future infections.
items.explanation: Patients who have had chancroid may have repeated infections, indicating no conferred protection.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: A serologic test for syphilis can distinguish cutaneous ulcers due to H. ducreyi from those due to yaws.
items.explanation: Serologic tests do not distinguish between ulcers caused by H. ducreyi and those caused by yaws.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Haemophilus ducreyi

items.statement: Contacts of patients with chancroid should be treated if they had sexual contact within the 10 days preceding symptom onset.
items.explanation: Contacts should be treated regardless of symptoms if they had recent sexual contact.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · DIAGNOSIS | Haemophilus ducreyi

items.statement: The prevalence of M. catarrhalis colonization increases with age.
items.explanation: The prevalence of colonization decreases steadily with age.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: M. catarrhalis is an encapsulated gram-negative diplococcus.
items.explanation: M. catarrhalis is an unencapsulated gram-negative diplococcus.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: H. influenzae type b (Hib) primarily causes disease in infants and children <6 years of age.
items.reason: Effective vaccine campaigns have made Hib rare in this age group.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the rarity is due to vaccination, not an inherent characteristic of the disease.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections | MICROBIOLOGY

items.assertion: The peak incidence of type b disease correlates with the antibody nadir.
items.reason: The level of maternally acquired serum antibody to PRP declines from birth to 6 months and remains low until ~2 or 3 years of age.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · B | IMMUNE RESPONSE

items.assertion: The mortality rate from Hib meningitis is approximately 5%.
items.reason: Hib meningitis has a high morbidity rate, with permanent sensorineural hearing loss in about 6% of survivors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the mortality rate.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Epiglottitis due to Hib primarily occurs in adults.
items.reason: In countries with Hib vaccine child immunization programs, epiglottitis now occurs primarily in adults.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.subtopic: HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.assertion: Haemophilus ducreyi has been classified in the genus Haemophilus.
items.reason: H. ducreyi requires X factor (hemin) for growth, similar to other Haemophilus species.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
items.reason: The ulcers caused by H. ducreyi generally do not show central granulating tissue.
items.explanation: A is false, but R is true; chancroid ulcers are generally not indurated.
items.reference: Haemophilus and Moraxella Infections · HAEMOPHILUS DUCREYI | MICROBIOLOGY | EPIDEMIOLOGY AND PREVALENCE | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS
